hero.title: A three-portal GERD management platform built from clinical discovery to delivery.
hero.summary: Between November 2024 and May 2025, Chethan N led the end-to-end design, development, and delivery of GastroTrack for Micro Labs Pharma at Techvaraha Solutions. The platform connected patients, gastroenterologists, and Micro Labs clinical operations into a single ecosystem for symptom tracking, treatment response, dashboards, alerts, and real-world evidence generation.

metrics.label: Delivery window
metrics.detail: 7 months from discovery to final delivery

metrics.label: Sprints
metrics.detail: Two-week sprint cadence across the build

metrics.label: Features shipped
metrics.detail: Across patient, doctor, and admin portals

problem: GERD affects approximately 7.6% to 30% of India's adult population depending on the region, yet patient management remains fragmented.
Patients often self-medicate with over-the-counter antacids, delaying diagnosis of serious complications such as Barrett's esophagus and esophageal strictures.
Gastroenterologists frequently track progress through handwritten notes or broad hospital EMRs that do not capture GERD-specific clinical parameters.
Pharma teams have limited visibility into real-world patient outcomes, therapy adherence, and response rates across their GERD treatment portfolio.
There was no structured mechanism for patient-reported outcomes such as symptom severity, trigger foods, lifestyle factors, or medication timing.

clientContext.title: Micro Labs Pharma
clientContext.body: Micro Labs Limited is a Bangalore-based pharmaceutical company ranked among India's top 20 pharma companies by domestic revenue. The project was commissioned by Micro Labs' clinical operations team to create a digital ecosystem that positioned the company as a GERD management thought leader while generating real-world evidence for its gastroenterology portfolio, including PPI therapies such as Pantoprazole, Rabeprazole, and Esomeprazole.

clinicalFoundation.definition: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease is a chronic condition where stomach acid frequently flows back into the esophagus. The underlying mechanism involves dysfunction of the lower esophageal sphincter, allowing gastric acid and bile to irritate esophageal tissue over time.
symptoms: Heartburn after eating, at night, or when lying down
Regurgitation with sour or bitter acid taste
Dysphagia or sensation of food being stuck
Chronic cough, laryngitis, and throat clearing
Sleep disruption due to nocturnal reflux

classification.description: One or more mucosal breaks <= 5mm, not extending between the tops of two mucosal folds
One or more mucosal breaks > 5mm, not extending between the tops of two mucosal folds
Mucosal breaks extending between the tops of two or more mucosal folds, involving < 75% of circumference
Mucosal breaks involving >= 75% of the esophageal circumference

diagnostics: Upper GI endoscopy with report uploads and LA grade mapping
24-hour ambulatory pH monitoring with DeMeester score tracking
Esophageal manometry for LES pressure and motility assessment
Barium swallow report upload and doctor annotations
riskFactors: Age, BMI, obesity status, and pregnancy status
Dietary habits including spicy food, caffeine, alcohol, citrus, and carbonated drinks
Medication history including NSAIDs, calcium channel blockers, and anticholinergics
Comorbidities including hiatal hernia, diabetes, asthma, and connective tissue disorders
treatmentPathways: Lifestyle modifications with personalized dietary and sleep recommendations
First-line PPI therapy tracking for dosage, duration, adherence, and response
Adjunct therapy including H2 receptor antagonists, prokinetics, and antacids
Surgical referral flags for refractory GERD after 12+ weeks of optimized PPI therapy
complications: Barrett's esophagus screening reminders for chronic symptoms and LA Grade C/D
Urgent endoscopy flags for progressive dysphagia and suspected stricture
Surveillance schedules for long-term Barrett's patients

role.headline: Single-threaded project owner from Day 1 to delivery.
role.summary: Operating from the Founder's Office at Techvaraha, Chethan reported directly to the CEO and acted as the primary interface across internal teams, gastroenterologists, and Micro Labs leadership.

areas.title: Product Definition and Strategy
items: Conducted clinical discovery with 6 gastroenterologists and shadowed 2 consultations.
Authored a 62-page PRD covering personas, features, data models, validation rules, and acceptance criteria.
Defined 3 portals, MVP scope, Phase 2 backlog, and user journey maps.

areas.title: Stakeholder Management
items: Managed VP-level client reviews, scope negotiation, and timeline expectations.
Built working relationships with 6 gastroenterologists for validation and feedback.
Translated clinical language into technical specifications for the engineering team.

areas.title: Team Leadership
items: Managed a 12-member cross-functional team across design, backend, BA, QA, and DevOps.
Ran daily standups, sprint planning, retrospectives, velocity tracking, and risk registers.
Reviewed every UI mockup, API contract, and QA test case before sign-off.

areas.title: Quality and Delivery
items: Defined healthcare data validation protocols and clinical value range checks.
Conducted UAT with doctors and Micro Labs stakeholders.
Managed go-live readiness across encryption, access control, backup, and rollback.

team.responsibilities: PRD, stakeholder management, standups, sprint planning, QA oversight, client presentations
Wireframes, high-fidelity mockups, design system, doctor-validated interface patterns
API development, database architecture, clinical data models, server infrastructure
Requirements documentation, data flow mapping, stakeholder requirement translation
Test cases, regression testing, clinical data validation, UAT coordination
CI/CD pipeline, deployment, monitoring, data backup protocols

stakeholders.name: Micro Labs VP
stakeholders.need: Final sign-off, RWE generation, competitive positioning, and scope decisions

stakeholders.name: Techvaraha CEO
stakeholders.need: Client relationship narrative, major presentations, and executive readiness

stakeholders.name: 2 Senior Gastroenterologists
stakeholders.need: Clinically credible displays, raw data access, and simplified summaries

stakeholders.name: Clinical Ops Team
stakeholders.need: Admin controls, training, documentation, and no-developer operation

stakeholders.name: Medical Representatives
stakeholders.need: A clear 3-minute value proposition for doctor onboarding

architecture.principle: Simple enough for a 55-year-old patient with moderate digital literacy.
features: 5-step onboarding with demographics, risk factors, symptom history, and BMI auto-calculation
Daily tracker for 7 symptoms on a 0-10 scale with trigger capture
Food and lifestyle diary with known GERD trigger flags
PPI adherence tracking with visual calendar, missed dose alerts, and refill reminders
Appointment management, visit summaries, lab results, and education library

architecture.principle: Give a 30-second overview, then let the doctor drill down.
features: Kanban patient board filtered by LA grade, treatment phase, adherence, and alerts
Individual dashboard with symptom trends, treatment response, adherence heatmap, diagnostic timeline, and risk summary
Aggregate analytics for severity distribution, response rates, adherence, complications, and demographics
Digital prescription generation and structured clinical notes

architecture.principle: Generate real-world evidence and manage the ecosystem.
features: Ecosystem dashboard for enrollment, active patients, doctors, geography, and trends
RWE reports for treatment response, adherence by drug, and dose patterns
Population demographics analytics for medical affairs and regulatory use
Doctor engagement tracking, user management, permissions, and audit trails

timeline.title: Discovery and Clinical Immersion
timeline.output: 62-page PRD with personas, feature specs, clinical rules, data model requirements, and wireframe sketches

timeline.title: Design and Architecture
timeline.output: Approved designs for 3 portals, database schema, API contracts, and doctor-validated patterns

timeline.title: Core Development
timeline.output: Authentication, onboarding, demographics, symptom tracker, medication adherence, and patient dashboard

timeline.title: Advanced Features
timeline.output: Doctor portal, patient board, diagnostic history, analytics, prescriptions, and admin foundation

timeline.title: Integration, QA, and Polish
timeline.output: Admin portal completion, full integration testing, bug fixes, security audit, and performance optimization

timeline.title: UAT and Delivery
timeline.output: Doctor and stakeholder UAT, final polish, user guides, admin training, deployment readiness, and final client demo

features.name: Smart Symptom Tracker
features.description: Daily logging of 7 symptoms on a 0-10 scale with trigger capture and correlation with meals and medication timing

features.name: Food and Trigger Diary
features.description: Meal logging with auto-flagging of 12 known GERD triggers and visual timeline of meals vs. symptoms

features.name: PPI Adherence Tracker
features.description: Calendar heatmap showing adherence percentage, missed dose alerts, refill reminders, and adherence trend line

features.name: Patient Board
features.description: Kanban-style overview filterable by LA Grade, treatment phase, adherence status, and alert flags

features.name: Clinical Analytics Dashboard
features.description: Severity distribution, response rates, adherence trends, complication alerts, and demographics

features.name: Complication Alert Engine
features.description: Rule-based flags for Barrett's screening, stricture evaluation, and surgical referral

features.name: RWE Report Generator
features.description: Aggregate treatment outcomes across all doctors and patients, exportable for medical affairs use

features.name: Ecosystem Dashboard
features.description: Enrollment trends, doctor engagement metrics, geographic distribution, and patient retention rates

crises.title: Unauthorized UX Redesign
crises.whatHappened: A UI/UX designer changed the symptom tracker after informal doctor feedback, breaking 3 backend APIs already in progress.
crises.resolution: Rolled back the change and created a formal change-control process requiring written sign-off, backend impact assessment, and validation from at least 2 doctors.
crises.outcome: Zero unauthorized design changes for the rest of the project and stabilized team velocity.

crises.title: Senior Doctor Data Display Conflict
crises.whatHappened: One senior doctor wanted raw 24-hour pH monitoring data while another wanted only the DeMeester composite score.
crises.resolution: Designed a two-layer interface: summary traffic-light view by default, with a View Raw Data toggle for the full 24-hour pH plot.
crises.outcome: Both doctors approved within 24 hours and became active advocates for the platform.

crises.title: VP Scope Change Mid-Sprint
crises.whatHappened: Micro Labs' VP requested comparative PPI effectiveness reporting after a gastroenterology conference.
crises.resolution: Scoped the feature in 48 hours, showed the 2-sprint effort and trade-off, and recommended building it because of its business value.
crises.outcome: The report became one of the most valued features for medical affairs presentations.

crises.title: Database Schema Bug
crises.whatHappened: Patients with multiple diagnostic tests of the same type only showed the latest result due to an incorrect uniqueness rule.
crises.resolution: Redesigned the schema around patient ID, test type, test date, and sequence number, migrated data, and retested affected flows.
crises.outcome: No data loss, caught before production, and fixed within 2 days.

crises.title: Frontend-Backend Integration Failures
crises.whatHappened: 11 of 42 API endpoints returned incorrect data or timed out when all 3 portals were connected.
crises.resolution: Ran a focused integration week, paired frontend and backend developers, triaged failures, and moved to twice-daily check-ins.
crises.outcome: All 11 failures resolved in 5 days and live API testing became mandatory every sprint.

technicalDecisions.title: Desktop-first, no mobile in MVP
technicalDecisions.decision: Doctors and Micro Labs ops teams primarily worked on desktops. A mobile build would have added roughly 40% to timeline and budget, so responsive and patient mobile work moved to Phase 2.

technicalDecisions.title: PostgreSQL over MongoDB
technicalDecisions.decision: Healthcare data had rigid relationships across patients, encounters, symptoms, medications, and tests. Relational constraints protected data integrity.

technicalDecisions.title: Role-based access control
technicalDecisions.decision: Patients saw only their own data, doctors saw only their assigned patients, and admins saw aggregate de-identified data. Every data access event was logged.

technicalDecisions.title: Rule-based alerts over ML
technicalDecisions.decision: Clinical alert logic needed to be transparent, auditable, and validated. ML was premature for a zero-to-one healthcare product.

outcomes: 3 fully functional portals delivered with 47 features.
14 two-week sprints completed across 7 months on the original timeline.
286 test cases executed with 99% final QA pass rate.
Dashboard load time optimized from 4.2 seconds to 1.8 seconds.
11 core database entities designed with relational integrity.
Role-based access control and audit trails implemented.
User guides, admin training, backup protocols, and rollback procedures completed.

lessons.title: Learn the domain before writing the PRD
lessons.body: Three weeks of GERD immersion saved months of rework because healthcare products cannot be built well without understanding disease, patient journey, and doctor workflow.

lessons.title: Influence without authority is the core skill
lessons.body: Senior doctors listened when product decisions were framed around making their 15-minute consultations more effective.

lessons.title: Say yes to the right scope changes
lessons.body: The PPI comparative report was scope change, but it created real client value. The key was making trade-offs explicit.

lessons.title: Build systems, not heroics
lessons.body: Change control, daily standups, sprint buffers, and blocker SLAs made the project resilient without depending on 18-hour days.

lessons.title: Healthcare products are three-sided markets
lessons.body: Patients, providers, and pharma all need clear value. The product that wins creates value across all three simultaneously.

alerts: 4 refractory GERD cases need surgical review
